runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for top U.S. earners, combining tax avoidance and real behavioral responses to changes in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Slemrod (behavioral responses; ETI/avoidance discussion)
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (taxable income elasticity evidence overview)
Gruber and Saez / Eissa and Liebman (ETI estimation context for high-income taxpayers)
runs.reasoningSummary: For high-income taxpayers, medium-run ETI that blends avoidance and real responses is typically thought to be positive and moderate-to-high, with plausible variation from relatively small responses to near-unit elasticity. I place the median around 0.45, with a long right tail reflecting stronger avoidance/realignment possibilities for some taxpayers under net-of-tax changes.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticities of taxable income for top earners in the US, combining avoidance and real (behavioral) responses to changes in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Slemrod (taxable income elasticity / response to net-of-tax incentives)
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (taxable income responses and ETI framework)
Mirrlees/optimal tax theory intuition on behavioral responses to marginal incentives
runs.reasoningSummary: For high-income taxpayers, I expect a moderate ETI in the medium run: enough to reflect both avoidance (shifting income, deductions) and real responses, but typically not so large that taxable income becomes nearly proportional to the net-of-tax rate. The distribution is right-skewed because in some tax reforms/contexts behavioral responsiveness appears stronger, yielding upper-tail elasticities approaching ~1.

The distribution is right-skewed because in some tax reforms/contexts behavioral responsiveness appears stronger, yielding upper-tail elasticities approaching ~1."}

runs.reasoningSummary: For high-income taxpayers, I expect a moderate positive ETI in the medium run once both avoidance and real responses are included. runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income (ETI) for top U.S. earners, incorporating both tax avoidance and real behavioral responses to changes in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Saez (early ETI framing and evidence for substantial elasticity among high earners)
Slemrod and coauthors (avoidance vs. real responses contributions to ETI)
Chetty et al. (discussion of medium-run behavioral margins and ETI magnitude)
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the median medium-run ETI for high-income U.S. taxpayers in the neighborhood of ~0.7, with plausible variability from modest responses (~0.3) to stronger combined avoidance/real responses (~1.3). This reflects the idea that top earners have meaningful flexibility via both reporting/avoidance and time-allocation/income shifting, but that the elasticity is not typically extremely large in the medium run.
